# Factors That Influence the Immune Response to Vaccination

### Affiliations

# Factors That Influence the Immune Response to Vaccination

### Authors

### Affiliations

## Abstract

There is substantial variation between individuals in the immune response to vaccination. In this review, we provide an overview of the plethora of studies that have investigated factors that influence humoral and cellular vaccine responses in humans. These include intrinsic host factors (such as age, sex, genetics, and comorbidities), perinatal factors (such as gestational age, birth weight, feeding method, and maternal factors), and extrinsic factors (such as preexisting immunity, microbiota, infections, and antibiotics). Further, environmental factors (such as geographic location, season, family size, and toxins), behavioral factors (such as smoking, alcohol consumption, exercise, and sleep), and nutritional factors (such as body mass index, micronutrients, and enteropathy) also influence how individuals respond to vaccines. Moreover, vaccine factors (such as vaccine type, product, adjuvant, and dose) and administration factors (schedule, site, route, time of vaccination, and coadministered vaccines and other drugs) are also important. An understanding of all these factors and their impacts in the design of vaccine studies and decisions on vaccination schedules offers ways to improve vaccine immunogenicity and efficacy.

**Keywords:**
antibodies; cellular; cytokines; humoral; immunization; immunoglobulin.

DESCRIPTION: The image illustrates different factors affecting the immune response to COVID-19 vaccines, including the mechanisms of mRNA, adenoviral vector, protein-based, and attenuated virus vaccines, along with their interactions at the cellular level involving antigen-presenting cells and spike proteins.

URL: https://www.frontiersin.org/files/Articles/809244/fimmu-12-809244-HTML/image_m/fimmu-12-809244-g001.jpg
DESCRIPTION: The layout illustrates the processes of innate and adaptive immune activation in response to viral exposure, showing how pathogen-associated molecular patterns trigger immune responses, leading to the formation of memory T and B lymphocytes that provide long-term immunity.

IMAGE 4:
URL: https://www.frontiersin.org/files/Articles/453977/fmicb-10-01305-HTML/image_m/fmicb-10-01305-g002.jpg
DESCRIPTION: The diagram illustrates the complex interactions between vaccines, the immune system, and microbiota, highlighting factors such as antigen dose, antibiotic treatment, and age that influence vaccine immune response.

IMAGE 5:
URL: https://sites.bu.edu/covid-corps/files/2020/10/Immune-response-final.png
DESCRIPTION: The illustration depicts the process of immune response to vaccines, highlighting factors like spike protein (antigen), the entry and replication of the virus, and the roles of antigen-presenting cells and helper CD4+ T-cells in mediating cell- and antibody-based immunity against COVID-19.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Immunosenescence affects older adults, reducing vaccine efficacy; while overall health, including comorbidities, can significantly impact immune response.
Genetic variations influence immune system function, leading to differences in antibody production and overall vaccine response among individuals.
Different vaccine platforms (e.g., mRNA, inactivated) elicit varying immune responses, affecting the duration and strength of immunity.
Optimal timing, including seasonal factors and age-related schedules, can enhance vaccine effectiveness and immune memory.
Adjuvants enhance vaccine immunogenicity by stimulating a stronger immune response, crucial for effective long-term protection.
